title: Due process and judicial compensation
sub_topics: Special Allowance for Justices and Judges (SAJ)

stances.claim: The Supreme Court's Gamboa v. Teves decision is procedurally void because the foreign investors most adversely affected by it were never summoned, served, or given an opportunity to be heard.
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional textual reading + appeal to procedural fairness

stances.claim: Due process under the Bill of Rights protects 'persons' — a term that includes foreign individuals and corporations — and therefore foreign investors cannot be deprived of property at bargain prices without being heard first.
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional textual reading + citation of Themistocles and Daniel Webster

stances.claim: Adequate, non-taxable judicial compensation is necessary to fill trial court vacancies and to discourage graft, and it should be funded by Congress through the national coffers rather than by raising docket fees on poor litigants.
stances.rhetorical_move: policy argument grounded in personal experience as Chief Justice

signature_phrases: a law that hears before it condemns, proceeds upon inquiry and renders judgment only after trial
to the chagrin of poor litigants
decision_framework_signals: applies procedural due process lens to corporate/foreign-investment regulation — argues the constitutional 'person' protection extends to foreign corporations
frames judicial compensation as instrumental to two ends: filling vacancies (efficiency) and reducing graft (integrity)
notable_anecdotes: implementing the doubled judicial compensation during his own tenure as Chief Justice to address the 600 trial court vacancies
